title: Vietnamese Banh Mi Sandwich

ingredients.name: baguette or small French bread rolls (4 individual)
visual_checks: crusty outside, soft inside; cut lengthwise without breaking through the back
ingredients.notes: crusty outside, soft inside; cut lengthwise without breaking through the back

subs.name: any crusty roll, ciabatta, or even a thick slice of toasted white bread in a pinch

ingredients.name: minced pork or chicken (for pork patties)

subs.name: 200 g firm tofu, sliced and pan-fried, for a vegetarian filling
subs.notes: marinate with same soy sauce mixture

subs.name: extra soy sauce for a fish-free version

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: 1 tbsp for pickling, 1 tbsp for meat
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp for pickling, 1 tbsp for meat

subs.name: lime juice for a brighter, more tropical pickle

steps.text: Quick-pickle the carrot: toss the julienned carrot with 1 tablespoon vinegar, 1 tablespoon sugar and a pinch of salt. Massage briefly and let sit for at least 15 minutes — the carrot will soften slightly and turn tangy. This is the essential acidic note in banh mi.

Make the pork/chicken patties: combine the minced meat with garlic, soy sauce, fish sauce, 1 tablespoon sugar, black pepper and a pinch of salt. Mix well with your hands, then shape into 8-10 small flat patties or short sausage shapes.

Heat oil in a frying pan or tawa on medium-high heat. Cook the patties for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through with no pink inside. They should be slightly caramelised on the outside from the sugar in the marinade.
If the bread is not fresh and crusty, warm it in a dry pan, tawa or oven for 3-5 minutes until the crust is crisp. Split lengthwise without cutting all the way through, leaving a hinge at the back.

Assemble the banh mi: spread a thin layer of butter or oil on both cut surfaces of the bread. Layer in the cooked patties, drained pickled carrot, cucumber strips, generous coriander sprigs and sliced green chilli. Press gently to compact.

Drizzle with the remaining 2 tablespoons of vinegar mixed with a pinch of sugar as a light dressing — this mimics the classic daikon and carrot pickle brine. Serve immediately while the bread is still crisp.

safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → 200 g firm tofu, sliced and pan-fried, for a vegetarian filling (marinate with same soy sauce mixture); bread → any crusty roll, ciabatta, or even a thick slice of toasted white bread in a pinch; fish-sauce → extra soy sauce for a fish-free version; vinegar → lime juice for a brighter, more tropical pickle
indian_kitchen_adaptation: French bread (baguette) is sold at bakeries in most Indian cities; pav (soft bread roll) can substitute in a pinch, though it is softer. A tawa works perfectly for pan-frying the patties. The quick-pickled carrot (gajar) with vinegar (sirka) and sugar is easy to make anywhere in India. Mince (keema) of chicken or pork is widely available; chicken keema at any Indian poultry shop is fresh and affordable.
